comparisons.name: KanCare Medicaid
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Multiple tiers (ABD basic, Nursing Home, HCBS waivers) with NFLOC for LTC; strict $2,000 single asset cap; no full income table by household size in sources; waiver slots limited causing waitlists; spousal/community protections vary marital status
gotchas: Kansas did not expand Medicaid; no coverage for low-income childless adults.
5-year look-back penalizes asset gifts/transfers for long-term care.
NFLOC required for LTC; basic ABD Medicaid doesn't need it but LTC assessment does.
Spousal rules complex: community spouse asset allowance ~$154,140 (2026), income disregard.
Medically Needy/Spenddown: pay medical bills to 'spend down' excess income.
Home equity limit applies if no intent to return.
Must report other insurance first.

comparisons.name: KanCare Frail Elderly Waiver (FE Waiver)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: No fixed income limit but patient liability of $2,982/month (2026); NFLOC via FAI assessment; statewide with waitlist due to slot limits; home equity exemption with $752,000 cap
gotchas: Must first qualify for KanCare Medicaid; waiver denial if not financially eligible upfront.[2]
60-month Look-Back Rule on asset transfers leads to penalty periods.[1]
Limited slots create waitlists; not guaranteed immediate access.[3]
Services require prior authorization via POC; no coverage for room/board.[4][5]
Home equity limit $752,000 (2026); intent to return must be documented.[1]

comparisons.name: KanCare PACE
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Limited to specific counties with multiple providers; no income/asset test unique to PACE (unlike standard Medicaid); requires state NFLOC certification; benefits identical across providers but access varies by region.
gotchas: Must live in specific PACE service area counties; not statewide[3][6].
Requires KDADS certification for nursing facility level of care, even if not on Medicaid[3][4].
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, certain Medicare plans, or hospice[2].
No financial eligibility test, but private pay premium if not dual eligible[2].
Must be able to live safely in community at enrollment; not for those already in nursing homes[2][3].
Documents from 2017; check providers for current details as program expansion may have occurred.

comparisons.name: Food Assistance Program (SNAP)

diffs.found: Monthly EBT card for food purchases (groceries, no hot foods/alcohol/tobacco). Amount based on net income, household size, deductions: e.g., max allotment for 2-person elderly household ~$546 minus 30% net income. Varies; use KS DCF calculator for exact[2][3].

novelFields.value: Kansas follows federal rules; most households have asset test, but households with elderly (60+) or disabled may be exempt depending on state policy (not explicitly waived in KS per sources). Countable assets include cash, bank accounts, stocks; exempt: home, retirement savings, life insurance cash value (if not income-producing), household goods, income-producing property (varies). Some states waive if income below FPL; check with DCF for KS specifics[1][2][4].
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: For households with a member 60 or older (elderly) or disabled in Kansas (Oct 1, 2025 - Sept 30, 2026): No gross income limit. Must meet net income limit (gross income minus deductions like medical expenses over $35/month for elderly/disabled, shelter costs, utilities). Gross income limits apply only if no elderly/disabled: 1 person $1695/month, 2 $2291, 3 $2887, 4 $3482, 5 $4079, 6 $4674, 7 $5270, +$595 each additional (130% FPL). Seniors (60+) only need to pass net income test[1][2][3].
novelFields.note: Benefits/eligibility vary by household size — we store a single number

novelFields.value: Administered uniformly by KS DCF, but local offices handle interviews/home visits; possible minor processing differences by office load[6][7]
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

value: Proof of ID
Social Security numbers
Income proof (Social Security award letters, pay stubs, pensions)
Medical expenses over $35/month for elderly/disabled
Rent/mortgage, utility bills for shelter deduction
Bank statements if assets apply
Proof of disability if claimed[2][6]
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: No gross income limit for households with elderly 60+ or disabled; net income only with big deductions for medical/shelter; benefits scale by household size and net income; statewide via DCF with local offices
gotchas: Kansas does not use broad-based categorical eligibility (BBCE), so stricter income tests than some states[5]
Many eligible seniors miss out; only ~half apply/enroll[1]
Include all household members who buy/prepare food together, even non-eligible[1]
Social Security/pensions count as income; medical costs over $35 deductible for net income[1][2][6]
Assets may still apply unless exempt; recent 2025 law changes to work/non-citizen rules (check updates)[3][4]
Authorized rep can apply for elderly/disabled[6]

comparisons.name: Low-Income Energy Assistance Program (LIEAP)

diffs.found: Specific dollar amounts not disclosed in available documentation; benefit amounts determined by household income, number of persons at address, type of dwelling, type of heating fuel, and utility rates[6]

novelFields.value: Not specified in available documentation; eligibility based on income, household size, available resources, and responsibility for payment of home energy costs[4].
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Applications submitted to designated DCF Regional LIEAP processing office for eligibility determination[5]; no specific regional variations in benefits or eligibility documented
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this
Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Signatures of all adults living in residence[2]
Proof of income (earned and unearned) for anyone living in residence[2]
Copy of all fuel bills (gas, electric, propane, etc.)[2]
Proof of child support payments received or court order[2]
If in subsidized housing, copy of rental agreement[2]
If primary heating costs included in rent, copy of rental agreement[2]
If utility bills in landlord's name, copy of lease agreement[2]
If applicable, VA award letter, SSA/SSI award letter, or pension award letter[6]
If claiming self-employment, complete copy of most recent tax return[6]
Provide copies of documents, not originals[6]
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: LIEAP is a federally funded program with annual application periods (typically January 20 – March 31). Benefits vary by household size, heating fuel type, dwelling type, and utility rates, but specific benefit amounts are not disclosed in public documentation. Eligibility is income-based (150% federal poverty level) with household size adjustments. The program counts all household members' income, which differs from other assistance programs. Tribal members are served separately through tribal LIHEAP programs. Processing time and waitlist information not publicly available. Application must include all household members and their income documentation[4][5][6].
gotchas: Application period is limited: 2026 period was January 20 – March 31[4]; application deadline is typically last business day of March, but varies by year[5]
LIEAP assistance is subject to available federal funding; not guaranteed[4]
No household may receive LIEAP more than once per program year[1]
Tribal members cannot receive both state LIEAP and tribal LIHEAP in same benefit year[1]
College students living in dorms are completely ineligible; students visiting home during breaks cannot be counted as household members[1]
All household members' income is counted, not just applicant's income; this differs from TANF and Food Assistance programs[5]
Applicant must be personally responsible for heating costs — if heating included in subsidized rent, household may not meet vulnerability requirements[5]
LIEAP eligibility differs from TANF and Food Assistance eligibility; household may qualify for one but not the other[5]
Legally incapacitated persons cannot apply on their own behalf[1]
Must provide signatures of all adults in residence[2]
comparisons.benefitVariesBy: household_size, heating fuel type, dwelling type, utility rates, and household income[6]

comparisons.name: Kansas Weatherization Assistance Program (K-WAP)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: This program's structure is complex due to multiple funding sources (DOE, LIEAP, Evergy utility partnership) with different income thresholds. Benefits are entirely in-kind (no cash payments). Priority tier system means elderly/disabled households get expedited service when waitlists exist. Geographic variation is significant — program is statewide but administered through regional providers with different service areas. Automatic income qualification for SSI/TANF/LIEAP recipients is a major pathway that bypasses standard income verification. No asset test simplifies eligibility for elderly on fixed incomes.
gotchas: Income limits vary significantly by funding source (150% vs. 60% of median vs. 200% of poverty level) — the actual limit depends on which funding stream your application uses.[1][4]
Automatic income qualification (SSI, TANF, LIEAP) bypasses income verification entirely — if elderly person receives any of these, they qualify regardless of other income.[1][2]
For renters: landlord must agree in writing AND commit to not raising rent for 2 years post-completion — this can be a barrier if landlord refuses.[6]
Utilities must be actively on — if service is disconnected, applicant is ineligible until reconnected.[3]
For homeowners: landlords are required to partially pay for furnace replacements (with exemptions for low-income landlords, public housing, emergency shelters).[1]
Program operates April to March annually, though applications accepted year-round.[1]
Waiting lists develop during high-demand periods; elderly and disabled households get priority but may still wait.[1]
Up to 15% of LIEAP block grant may be allocated to weatherization — this means funding is limited and varies by year.[1]
Regional provider variation means eligibility details, processing times, and available services may differ by county — must contact local provider for specifics.[2][7]
comparisons.officialSource: https://content.dcf.ks.gov/ees/keesm/current/keesm13500.htm (Kansas Department of Children and Family Services)

comparisons.name: Senior Health Insurance Counseling for Kansas (SHICK)

diffs.found: Free, confidential, one-on-one counseling on Medicare (Parts A-D), Medicare Supplement (Medigap), Medicare Advantage, prescription drug coverage (Part D), Extra Help, long-term care insurance, claims/appeals, supplemental rate comparisons, employer group plans, Medicaid, employment/disability Medicare info; public education presentations; available Monday-Friday, typically 8:30am-3pm or 8am-5pm depending on location[1][2][3][4][5][7][8]

novelFields.value: No asset limits or tests apply[2][8]
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Delivered through local Area Agencies on Aging and community partners with regional counselors and contact numbers; availability and hours may vary slightly by location (e.g., Sedgwick 8:30am-3pm, state office 8am-5pm)[1][3][5][6][8]
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

value: Medicare card, current insurance documents, prescription lists recommended for personalized counseling but not mandatory[7]
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: no income/asset test; volunteer-delivered statewide counseling service via local Area Agencies on Aging; part of federal SHIP network
gotchas: Counselors are volunteers, not insurance agents, and unaffiliated with industry—cannot sell plans[1][2][8]
Peak demand during Medicare Open Enrollment (Oct-Dec) may limit immediate appointments[6]
Not a healthcare provider or financial aid program—only counseling and education[2][7]
Must schedule appointments; no walk-ins specified[1][4][5]

comparisons.name: Family Caregiver Support Program

diffs.found: Support services including respite care (e.g., K-RAD subsidizes up to $1,000/year per care recipient for respite, paid to providers); in-home respite by paid workers/volunteers; referrals to personal care, homemaker services, adult day care via KanCare Medicaid; education, resources, and support[3][8][9]. No fixed hours or statewide payment to caregivers specified; varies by Medicaid waiver.

novelFields.value: No specific asset limits detailed; Medicaid programs assess countable assets excluding primary home, one vehicle, and certain exemptions, but exact rules program-specific and not enumerated[6].
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Varies county-to-county (e.g., personal care/homemaker via local providers); different AAAs handle intake (e.g., ECKAAA in Garnett area); funding/wait times differ[2][3][4].
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Possible due to limited funding (e.g., K-RAD no awards guaranteed); regional variation likely[3].
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of care recipient's Medicaid eligibility or nursing facility level of care.
Documentation of diagnosis (e.g., Alzheimer's for K-RAD).
Background check, health exam, or training may be required for paid roles[2].
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Decentralized via 11 Area Agencies on Aging with county variations; no fixed income/asset tables or statewide caregiver payment; tied to Medicaid waivers requiring nursing facility level of care; respite-focused with caps like K-RAD.
gotchas: Not a single uniform program; support delivered via network of AAAs, Medicaid waivers (e.g., Frail Elderly), and targeted initiatives like K-RAD—must contact local AAA for specifics[3][8][9].
No guaranteed statewide payment to family caregivers; relies on Medicaid eligibility and consumer-directed options which require care recipient qualification[5][6][7].
Funding limited (e.g., K-RAD $1,000 cap, first-come basis); may involve background checks/trainings for paid roles[2][3].
Often confused with VA programs; Kansas-specific is non-VA[1][9].

comparisons.name: Legal Services for Seniors
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: No income/asset test; priority-based for at-risk seniors; delivered statewide via hotline and local projects with Area Agencies on Aging; pro bono volunteer-driven.
gotchas: No income eligibility required for hotline, but full representation prioritizes at-risk/low-income seniors.[2][3]
Not all cases accepted; may be referred to private pro bono attorneys if conflicts or barriers exist.[2]
Civil issues only; focuses on elder abuse, benefits access, rights—not criminal matters.
Volunteers handle hotline; expect varying attorney availability.[2]

comparisons.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program

diffs.found: Independent advocacy including identifying, investigating, and resolving complaints; providing information on long-term care services and resident rights; representing residents before agencies for administrative, legal, or other remedies; regular presence in facilities to observe care and ensure access; addressing issues like improper discharges, medication errors, abuse, staffing, food service, daily care, privacy, and dignity.

novelFields.value: No asset limits; no financial tests apply.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Delivered through regional ombudsman offices with local staff and volunteers; find specific office via https://www.ombudsman.ks.gov/find-your-ombudsman. No specified differences in services, but contact and hours may vary by region.
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

value: No documents needed to request services; resident consent required for complaint investigations. Provide details of concern (e.g., facility name, issue description).
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: no income test; advocacy-only for facility residents; regional delivery model; open to all without eligibility barriers
gotchas: Not a financial or direct service program—provides advocacy only, not healthcare, housing, or payments. Requires resident consent for actions. Distinct from KanCare Ombudsman (for Medicaid issues). Volunteers need training and screening, but residents do not apply to 'qualify'—anyone can contact for help. Not for community-dwelling elderly outside facilities.
